text.title: Citation Contract Back to Source Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Citation Contract Back to Source application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Citation MUST follow the literal format `[faion:<slug>@<version>]` with `<slug>` lowercase kebab-case and `<version>` semver. Variants ('faion-slug-v1', 'Faion: Slug') are rejected.
rule.rationale: Stable parseable format is the only way downstream tools can detect and resolve the citation reliably.

rule.statement: Verifier MUST resolve every citation against the local faion content store. Citations that do not resolve are rejected as fabricated.
rule.rationale: LLMs reliably fabricate plausible citations; without resolution, the contract is decorative not real.

rule.statement: Citation MUST include a concrete version (semver) that exists in the content store. Floating ('latest', '*') or missing versions are rejected.
rule.rationale: Floating citations let the cited content shift under the reader; pinning the version preserves the reference.

rule.statement: Agent MUST emit a citation any time it quotes, paraphrases, or relies on faion content. Missing citations on used content are rejected.
rule.rationale: Silent reuse breaks the attribution loop; the rule is on by default in the schema, not opt-in.

rule.statement: Verifier MUST surface failed citations to the agent operator (log + visible warning), not silently strip them. Hidden failures are rejected.
rule.rationale: Silently stripping failures makes fabrications look like 'no citation needed'; visible failure forces resolution.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
